sourceTrail.title: Apophenia as the Disposition to False Positives: A Unifying Framework for Openness and Psychoticism
sourceTrail.note: Useful for treating apophenia as over-detection of signal rather than as mere oddity or superstition.

sourceTrail.title: Belief in the Law of Small Numbers
sourceTrail.note: A classic starting point for why people expect small samples and short runs to look too representative.

sourceTrail.title: The Seven Sins of Memory: An Update
sourceTrail.source: Journal of Applied Research in Memory and Cognition
sourceTrail.note: A clear review source that situates consistency bias within the broader family of reconstructive memory errors.

sourceTrail.title: Assimilation and Contrast Effects in Reactions to Communication and Attitude Change
sourceTrail.source: Journal of Abnormal and Social Psychology
sourceTrail.note: A classic early anchor for context-driven shifts in judgment where nearby comparisons bend evaluation.

sourceTrail.title: Egocentric Biases in Availability and Attribution
sourceTrail.note: The classic source behind contribution overestimation and first-person availability effects in shared outcomes.

sourceTrail.title: Creating False Memories: Remembering Words Not Presented in Lists
sourceTrail.source: Journal of Experimental Psychology: Learning, Memory, and Cognition
sourceTrail.note: One of the most widely taught experimental demonstrations that confident recollection can be manufactured by structure and suggestion.

sourceTrail.title: Just between Dr. Language and I
sourceTrail.note: Arnold Zwicky's original coining of the term is still the clearest short statement of why noticing surges can masquerade as prevalence.

sourceTrail.title: Belief in the Law of Small Numbers
sourceTrail.note: The classic law-of-small-numbers framing helps explain why short-run imbalance gets treated as if it must soon self-correct.
